name: Gábor Szabó
abstract: Gábor István Szabó (Hungarian pronunciation: [ˈgaːbor ˈiʃtvaːn ˈsɒboː]; March 8, 1936 – February 26, 1982) was a Hungarian-American guitarist whose style incorporated jazz, pop, rock, and Hungarian music.

concerts.label: Gábor Szabó, Laurindo Almeida, and John Fahey, Guitars

about_quotes.speaker: John Abercrombie
about_quotes.evidence: I opened up the Berklee catalogue and there was a picture of Gabor Szabo from Hungary, standing there with a Gretch guitar. I said I relate. I can go here, you know, this guy plays electric guitar and he’s studying at this school.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Abercrombie interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, April 19, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Jerry Gordon
about_quotes.evidence: Gabor Szabo who played psychedelically you know, he had been a Hungarian freedom fighter when the Russians came into Hungary and then split.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jerry Gordon interviewed by Monk Rowe and Christian Goodwillie, via Zoom, June 30, 2021

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: I remember the name. I remember him. But I don't remember too much recording with him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Onaje Allan Gumbs
about_quotes.evidence: He, Cal Tjader and Gabor Szabo. Unfortunately all three of them have passed away.
about_quotes.doc_title: Onaje Allan Gumbs interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Chico Hamilton
about_quotes.evidence: Gabor left and Larry and Arnie came on the band and that’s how we did The Dealer.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chico Hamilton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
